name: Cooking with Heat
tagline: Applying controlled heat to food to improve safety, digestibility, and calorie extraction
problem:
Many foods are marginal without heat: proteins can harbour pathogens; some plants contain heat-labile toxins or enzyme inhibitors; starches in tubers and grains are less accessible raw. Chewing and digestion are metabolically expensive — heat denatures proteins, gelatinises starches, and breaks down cell walls, changing what calories a human gut can absorb per hour.

Cooking also concentrates flavours and enables combining ingredients into stews that homogenise nutrients across a group — a social technology as much as a chemical one.

The engineering problem is not merely temperature peaks but controlled heat transfer without burning, efficient fuel use, and containers that survive repeated thermal cycling. That couples cooking tightly to ceramics and later metals.
overview:
Cooking methods map to heat transfer modes. Direct radiation from coals or flame sears surfaces — good for Maillard browning, easy to overcook interiors. Conduction through stones (hot-rock boiling) heats liquids in organic containers that would burn in flame. Convection in earth ovens and steam pits circulates moist heat evenly. Immersion boiling in pottery is the gateway to grains and legumes at scale.

Temperature bands matter: pasteurisation concepts (time at temperature) reduce pathogen risk though premodern cooks used heuristics — foam, colour, smell. For plant toxins, specific knowledge replaces thermometers: slow simmering versus rapid boil changes outcomes for some cyanogenic or lectin-rich foods.

Fuel efficiency is a design variable: insulated pits reuse heat; lids reduce evaporative losses; stacking pots saves charcoal. Ash and soot management affects food taste and long-term respiratory health — another reason ventilation design belongs with cooking.

Nutritionally, cooking can reduce some heat-sensitive vitamins while increasing bioavailability of others; the net depends on diet composition. The civilisational net effect has been overwhelmingly enabling for population density.

principles.name: Denaturation and gelatinisation
principles.explanation: Heat unfolds proteins and swells starch granules, increasing digestibility and altering texture.

principles.name: Thermal death time for microbes
principles.explanation: Pathogen reduction is a function of temperature and duration — higher heat shortens required time.

principles.name: Moist versus dry heat
principles.explanation: Water limits surface temperature to ~100 °C at sea level, favouring even heating; dry air allows browning reactions above boiling.

principles.name: Heat capacity of cooking media
principles.explanation: Water and stone store more heat per mass than air — good for stable simmer after fire adjustment.

components.name: Heat source
components.criticalNote: Prefer coals for steady heat; flame impingement causes soot and uneven cooking

components.name: Cooking vessel
components.position: On trivet, in coals, or suspended
components.madeFrom: Pottery, later metal; organic bowls for hot-rock boiling
components.criticalNote: Thermal shock cracks pottery — preheat gently

components.name: Lid / cover
components.function: Traps steam, speeds heating, reduces fuel
components.criticalNote: Sealed lids can boil over explosively — vent if needed

components.name: Stirring and serving tools
components.function: Moves food for even heating; avoids local scorch
components.criticalNote: Avoid toxic species for spoons — some woods are irritant

rawMaterials.name: Water
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Recovered humidity condensate — must be purified
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Clarify and boil if biological risk

rawMaterials.name: Food ingredients
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Knowledge of local toxins is mandatory preprocessing

buildSteps.title: Establish a cooking fire layout
buildSteps.description: Rake a bed of coals; place adjustable pot support (stones, trivet) for air access. Keep a separate small flame zone for searing if needed.
buildSteps.warningNote: River stones with trapped moisture can explode — use dry dense stones

buildSteps.title: Pre-treat hazardous foods
buildSteps.description: Soak, ferment, or multiple-change boiling for known toxic plants per regional ethnobotany. When uncertain, do not eat — this step is knowledge-gated.
buildSteps.warningNote: Wild mushroom misidentification is not fixable by cooking

buildSteps.title: Bring to heat using appropriate mode
buildSteps.description: For grains/legumes, simmer until tender — hardness of water and altitude change times. For meats, ensure time-at-temperature heuristics your culture trusts.
prerequisiteTools: pottery or metal pot when available
buildSteps.warningNote: Undercooked pork and wild game carry parasite risk in many regions

buildSteps.title: Hold and rest
buildSteps.description: Thick stews continue cooking off heat due to thermal mass; meats rest to redistribute juices in culinary terms — in survival terms, reduces immediate scald risk.
buildSteps.warningNote: Spoilage risk remains — cool quickly if storing

buildSteps.title: Clean vessels
buildSteps.description: Scrape char, scour with sand, rinse hot. Residual oils go rancid; biofilms harbour pathogens.
buildSteps.warningNote: Thermal shock — do not plunge hot pottery into cold water

history.event: Anatomical and archaeological arguments propose increased meat processing and possible cooking correlates with smaller teeth and reduced gut size.
Pottery spreads cooking of grains and legumes, tying cuisine to sedentary storage.
Pasteurisation experiments help formalise heat treatment as a scientific countermeasure to microbial spoilage — a foundation for modern time–temperature safety practice.

inventors.name: Louis Pasteur
inventors.contribution: Demonstrated heat treatment and microbial causation of spoilage — foundation of pasteurisation science

impact:
Cooking underwrote denser populations and shorter feeding times, freeing hours for tool maintenance and travel. It is prerequisite to many preservation chains (though distinct from fermentation).

Industrial descendants include retorts, extrusion, and precision sous-vide — but the Codex conserves the campfire truth: heat plus container plus knowledge equals transformed biology.
